symbols: floodwaters — divine judgment purging the earth of wickedness
cascading waterfall — the overwhelming, unstoppable nature of God's wrath
naked bodies — humanity stripped of all pretense and protection before divine justice
outstretched arms — desperate, unanswered pleas contrasting with Noah's obedient faith
dark sky — the closing of the heavens' windows (Gen 7:11) and the extinguishing of hope
timber/raft — futile human efforts at self-salvation apart from God's appointed means (the Ark)
description: A harrowing scene of the biblical Deluge shows dozens of nude and semi-clothed human figures struggling against rising floodwaters. In the foreground, bodies are tangled together on what appears to be a raft or debris, some reaching upward in desperation, others already lifeless. A massive cascade of white water pours over a ledge in the middle distance, sweeping more figures to their doom. In the far background, additional groups cling to higher ground as the dark, rain-laden sky presses down. The palette is muted -- grays, browns, and pale flesh tones -- creating an atmosphere of desolation.
theological_description: The Flood (Gen 6:5-8:22) represents the most comprehensive act of divine judgment in the Old Testament prior to the eschatological promises. God saw that 'every inclination of the thoughts of the human heart was only evil all the time' (Gen 6:5) and resolved to unmake what He had made, returning creation to the primordial waters of Genesis 1:2. Tissot's focus on the drowning masses rather than the Ark forces the viewer to confront the terrible reality of judgment apart from grace. These are those who were warned by Noah, the 'preacher of righteousness' (2 Pet 2:5), and refused to enter the Ark. The New Testament draws explicit typological connections: Peter compares the Flood waters to baptism (1 Pet 3:20-21), wherein the old self drowns and the new self is raised; Jesus compares the days of Noah to the time before His Second Coming, when people will be eating and drinking, oblivious to approaching judgment (Matt 24:37-39). The cascading waterfall evokes the 'fountains of the great deep' bursting open (Gen 7:11), a reversal of the separation of waters in creation. The image is a sobering reminder that God's patience, though vast, has its appointed limit, and that salvation is found only through the means God Himself provides.

scripture_refs.reason: The Flood prevails over the earth, all flesh with the breath of life dies -- the direct narrative depicted.
God's grief over human wickedness and His resolve to destroy -- the theological cause of the Deluge.
The fountains of the great deep burst open and the windows of heaven were opened.
As in the days of Noah, so will be the coming of the Son of Man -- eschatological parallel.
The Flood as a type of baptism: water that destroys the old world and saves through the Ark.
God did not spare the ancient world but preserved Noah, a herald of righteousness.
The deep (tehom) of creation returns -- the Flood as an un-creation event.
By faith Noah built an ark and condemned the world -- the absent Ark heightens the condemnation of unbelief.
